title: Tall Corner Media Console
summary: A tall, narrow media cabinet built to fit a corner, with open component shelves up top and doored storage below.
description:
Corner furniture lives or dies on the face frame, because a corner cabinet has no adjacent piece to hide a crooked edge against — it's on display from two directions at once. Build the carcass first, then square up the face frame before you attach it; a face frame glued on out of square telegraphs through the whole front of the piece.

The open shelves on top are sized for game consoles and cable boxes and are left doorless on purpose, for remote-control line of sight. The lower section gets doors, and you have a real choice there: overlay doors (built slightly larger than the opening, mounted with overlay hinges) are more forgiving to install and easier to align, while inset doors look cleaner but demand that you measure your actual openings — not the plan's stated numbers — and build to a consistent 1/8" gap all around.

tools.note: For breaking down the plywood sheets into the ripped shelf strips.
For the 45° miters on the decorative top trim.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into 15-3/4" strips for the shelves — referred to as "1x16" boards in the cut list.

materials.name: Pine, 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Substitute ripped-down plywood strips if you have a table saw.

cutList.note: Ends cut to fit the corner angle.
Both ends 45° off square, NOT parallel, long-point measurement.
Both ends 45° off square, NOT parallel, long-point measurement.
Both ends 45° off square, NOT parallel, long-point measurement.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Break the 3/4" plywood sheet into three 15-3/4"-wide strips first, since every shelf part comes from them. Cut four shelves at 48" with their ends trimmed to the corner angle, one centre shelf at 20", and two dividers at 10-1/2"; each 48" shelf needs its own strip because two 48" parts plus the 1/8" kerf will not fit on a 96" rip (48 + 1/8 + 48 = 96-1/8"), so the four shelves alone take four strips — more than one 4x8 sheet yields when ripped to 15-3/4" (three strips). Buy a second sheet, or rip the 20" centre shelf and two 10-1/2" dividers from offcuts of a second sheet; do not plan on three strips covering all seven parts. From the 1x6 cut two sides at 47-1/4", both off the single 8 ft board. From the 1x2s cut four back supports at 47-1/4", two side face-frame pieces at 47-1/4" and three shelf face-frame trim pieces at 46-1/2" — that uses all five 8 ft 1x2s with very little to spare. From the 1/4" plywood cut the centre back panel 24-1/2" x 47-1/4" and two side back panels 13" x 47-1/4". Leave the 1x4 top trim, the base moulding and the 1x3 door frames uncut for now; all three are fitted to the finished carcass in their own steps. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the top shelves
steps.body: Build the two upper shelves that hold the media components — a pocket hole jig makes this fastest, but countersunk screws or a finish nailer with glue both work fine. If you're using a pocket hole jig, drill every pocket hole across the whole project now, before assembly starts — pocket holes are much easier to drill into a flat board than into a partly-built cabinet.

steps.title: Attach the top and bottom
steps.body: Attach the top shelf from underneath with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, and attach the bottom shelf from underneath with 2" countersunk screws — a pocket screw is very hard to drive into the bottom shelf once the cabinet is closed up, so use plain countersunk screws there instead.

steps.title: Lay out and attach the sides
steps.body: Hold the two 47-1/4" side panels edge to edge with their ends flush and mark every shelf location across both at once — the shelves are 15-3/4" deep and any drift between the two sides shows up as a tilted shelf. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the sides at each shelf line, then attach with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, working from the bottom shelf up. Check the diagonals across the open front once the second shelf is in. A corner cabinet is on display from two directions at once, so a rack that would hide against a flat wall shows on both faces here.

steps.title: Add the back panels
steps.body: Fasten the four 47-1/4" 1x2 back supports to the back edges of the shelves first — they give the thin panels something solid to land on. Then attach the three back pieces over them: the 24-1/2"-wide centre panel across the back of the corner, and a 13"-wide panel on each side, all 47-1/4" tall. Nail through with 1-1/4" finish nails into the supports and the shelf edges, keeping the panel edges tight to each other. The shelves are open, so any gap between panels is visible straight through the cabinet.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame as its own assembly first, using 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws — or attach each board directly to the carcass with nails and glue if you'd rather skip a separate sub-assembly. A corner cabinet has no neighboring piece to hide a crooked face frame against, so take the extra minute to check it square before it's permanently on.

steps.title: Add base moulding
steps.body: Cut the 5-1/4" base moulding from the 6 ft stick and wrap it round the base, mitering the outside corners at 45° for a clean return. Cut the front piece first and the two returns against it, and dry-fit all three before any glue — you have one 6 ft stick and no second chance at a miter. Nail it on with 1-1/4" finish nails into the carcass, keeping the bottom edge flat on the floor so the cabinet does not rock on its own trim.

steps.title: Build the decorative top
steps.body: The 3-1/2"-wide 1x4 top trim wraps the cabinet as a mitered ring: two side pieces at 50-1/2", a back-right piece at 50-1/2", a back-left at 43-1/2" and a front at 36-1/2". The cut list marks the last three as having both ends cut 45° off square and NOT parallel, given as long-point measurements — so set the saw, cut one end, then flip the board over to cut the other, or you will get a parallelogram instead of a trapezoid. Dry-fit the whole ring on the cabinet before you commit to any of it. It is not structural, so glue and 1-1/4" finish nails are plenty.

steps.title: Build and hang the doors
steps.body: For overlay doors, build each door slightly larger than its opening and hang with overlay hinges — this is the more forgiving option and easier to align. For inset doors, measure your ACTUAL openings (don't trust a plan number over your own cabinet) and build to a consistent 1/8" gap on all sides. Either way, build the door frames, add plywood door backs, and hang with cabinet hinges.

images.alt: Tall corner media console with open component shelves above and a doored cabinet below
